text: I was very surprised by flywheel efficiency, it is stated that production models with magnetic bearings and sealed in vacuum should have 90% efficiency. The 1 thing I can not find an answer to though - how long charge can be kept? I understand that of course momentum will fall with time and speed will decrease, partly due to not perfect vacuum partly to earth's rotation, how much exactly? Say if I charge 1 state of and art vacuum sealed magnetic bearing flywheel with 1kW of energy, how long till it be 80% so 800W and 0.8 of the speed, not even touching it? Can it store say 80% of the charge for 3 winter months till our solar panel can kick back in xD?

author: @mlewisham3973
text:
Cement is the source of about 8% of the world's carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions, according to think tank Chatham House.

If the cement industry were a country, it would be the third largest emitter in the world - behind China and the US. It contributes more CO2 than aviation fuel (2.5%) and is not far behind the global agriculture business (12%).

author: @AdricM
text: made one with my dad took the tire off,  and wound old copper wire around it until it was about 11lbs finished it off with epoxy  which we balanced using plasticine, (modeling clay)    then we had a gob big enough to balance,  we weighed the putty and mixed up just a pinch more epoxy (by weight)  expecting some would stick to the cup/mixing stick.    we got it pretty good that way.  and did other minor adjusts by drilling out some of the epoxy/wire in 2 or 3 spots.  thing would spin for ages.  lots of gyroscopic force.

Nice - brings to mind Lucien Yeoman's concrete lathe designs from 1915/16.
Any idea what the maximum safe speed for one of these flywheels would be? I'm guessing it's highly dependent on curing time.

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text: There is NO 'safe speed' for rotating non-reinforced concrete, regardless of curing time.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am sorry mate but I think you are wrong here - concrete flywheels are being tested for green energy storage at thousands of RPM - it all depends on the containment - actually in this flywheel it's not that bad as if you think about it you have wrapped the concrete in a steel band

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it would do mate but as I said to biker there is in fact a lot of interest in concrete flywheels - if you are interested check out some of the designs - search term is concrete flywheel

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Agreed. But note 'containment'. And I did say 'non-reinforced'. Concrete at Thousands of RPM? Scary. I wonder at the overall  'green' of concrete over say stacked steel discs cut from rolled plate or even cast steel discs (from scrap). Plus the density issues. And 'aging'.And consistency/certification/testing etc Plenty of things to consider.
Thanks for sharing.

author: @richardgregory4640
text: Now that, is a good idea! Steel reinforced concrete flywheel with built in bearing axel, chain drive and freewheel hub. Super cheap too.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: now you describe it that way it does seem good lol - cheers mate

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text: Steel 'Oil-drum' full of concrete, and car-axle bearings; tons of stored energy (pun intended). Or 'scrap' steel plates and same/similar bearings, higher density of steel makes more energy storage in a smaller volume, and its way stronger. don't under-estimate the danger of a bursting flywheel (add fibre and/or chicken wire to the concrete). An oxy-gas cutter on a radius arm cuts really well balanced steel discs in one pass.

replies.author: @lylehintz7275
replies.text:
@bikerfirefarter7280 
What mode would this flywheel explode?  Just asking to get an idea how fast this flywheel could turn for energy storage.

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text: @lylehintz7275 A flywheel stores energy at any speed of rotation, but the stored energy is related to V-squared so faster is better, and therein lies the danger. The mode of 'explosion' is generally catastrophic for brittle flywheel materials, chunks large and/or small flying at speed mostly in the plane of rotation. Materials of high density and with high tensile strength are preferred. Brittle materials confined within structures that can rupture are very risky and need robust outer cases capable of retaining the fragments and absorbing all the energy. Ideally the flywheel material itself, IF it fails, should disintegrate into very tiny particles, and the fracturing process should also absorb all the stored energy.

I don't know about Canterbury, but here in California, you can occasionally get a barbell set just for carrying it away. :)

author: @poosmate
text: Cool!  But wouldn't it just be easier to just use the wheel?  Am I missing something?  Love what you do, keep it up!  Take care, Poo

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: Without the weight it wouldn't be a flywheel to continue the momentum.

replies.author: @matthewellisor5835
replies.text:
Think of it as a battery for angular momentum.
Assuming somewhere around mid sixties cm Dia., at just 100RPM:
For a 500g ring you'd have about 3 Joules stored.
For a 25kg disk, it would be more like 75 Joules! 
Basically, for the pedal-power, it lets you use your muscles at peak efficiency and rest between power-strokes. Just like a 4-stroke ICE only makes power on one-half of one revolution and has to coast the other three-quarters of the time.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: a flywheel needs mass mate - all you are doing with the concrete is adding mass - a lot of it!

author: @Peter_Schluss-Mit-Lustig
text: Doesn't this flywheel have a lot of dead-weight moving too close to the center to be efficient?

replies.author: @thefirstmissinglink
replies.text: But it's cheap, and easy to make. This could easily be adapted by placing it all on a flat surface and putting a cardboard Rim around it to thicken the outside.

replies.author: @Peter_Schluss-Mit-Lustig
replies.text: @thefirstmissinglink or you could remove the dead-mass on the inside by planting holes inside the wheel

replies.author: @Peter_Schluss-Mit-Lustig
replies.text: @colinhamer6506 but a lot less so

replies.author: @karlmyers6518
replies.text: I think the point is a very easy and quick process to make a flywheel.  You may get gains from moving mass changing shapes or changing bearings but this is a 10min flywheel that you could reproduce time and time again. I'd happily watch a video of an improved version if you want to do the testing 😉

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it does - but it is cheap and easy - so if the choice is no flywheel because of difficulty and expense or this flywheel then this flywheel is far more efficient than no flywheel - of course if you have a cast iron wheel - use that
